text: Thanks very much. I'd like to thank Sages and Dr. Salke for the invitation to speak.
Here's my disclosure slide.

When Dr. Salke asked me to give this talk, the short title is What's in Your Program? Laparoscopic Restorative Protoclectomy or Protoclectomy in J-Patch Anal Anastomosis, but then he gave me an addendum.
Do patients really benefit and how does rectal dysplasia influence the procedure?
So I'm going to try and address both of those by looking a little bit at background, going over some of the results. Steve Wexner has already gone over a lot of those that are pertinent to this.
How does rectal dysplasia or any dysplasia affect the procedure? And then show some videos with some tips and tricks.

As I think you may have appreciated from Dr. Wexner's video, doing a proctocolectomy is a difficult procedure. You've got a large specimen. You're working in multiple quadrants of the abdominal cavity. There are large blood vessels that need to be controlled.
And when we're doing a J-pouch, we have to reestablish bowel continuity.

With ulcerative colitis, the extent of disease means that we have to take out the entire colon and the rectum. The patients are often immunosuppressed and malnourished, and the procedure's made more complex if there is associated cancer or dysplasia.
So in some of the early series, we weren't seeing very much in the way of benefits at all. The OR time was increased, there was increased morbidity, and there didn't appear to be any advantages.
Dr. Wexner himself did one of the earlier studies back in nineteen ninety-two, and this essentially was a feasibility study. It was to show that the procedure was feasible, which it was.
But as you see, and as he's already shown you, the OR times are longer, the duration of ileus was longer, and length of stay was longer.

It was not until, uh, two thousand, and I think Peter Marcello was actually one of the first people to show that there could actually be advantages from this, and basically the main one was that the, uh, length of stay in the bottom row there was shortened by one day.

Others have shown similar findings here. Key, uh, looked at complications which appeared similar to previously published, uh, reviews in the literature, although this was a retrospective review without any controls.
Hasagawa, another series of eighteen patients, similar findings.

Here, Dunker had a case control study, particularly looking at functional outcome and body image, and although there was no difference in quality of life or satisfaction, there were significantly higher satisfaction rates with the cosmetic results for a laparoscopic approach compared with open.

Some of our data from Mayo Clinic, they had a hundred laparoscopic J-pouch patients matched with two hundred open and looked at safety, feasibility, and short-term outcomes. Within the laparoscopic group, there were seventy-five laparoscopic assisted and twenty-five hand assisted.
And here you'll see that the patients were well matched for body mass index, so we're not just cherry picking the skinny ones to do them laparoscopically. Oh, our time, as you've seen, is longer.

Post-op complications were very similar, but benefits were seen in time to return of bowel function, regular diet, duration of narcotic use, and length of stay, a three-day difference with a median of four days for laparoscopic group versus seven for open.

What was interesting, however, was that looking at the data in a little more detail, there were no benefits to the hand assist. It actually took significantly longer to do the hand-assisted approach, and this may possibly have been that there were four surgeons doing those twenty-five hand assist cases, and there was a learning curve involved.
It may reflect the difficulty, actually, of doing the rectal dissection through a small fan and steel incision.

So going back a little bit and looking at some of the actual technical tips, some of the early studies which showed how difficult it was, we went back to look to see if we could do this in a more simple fashion.
The initial descriptions used seven incisions, five port sites, a separate fan and steel incision for extraction of the specimen, and then a separate stoma site as well.

And it was noted in the original paper that perhaps the prolonged duration of post-operative ileus was because of the amount of traction that was used to take the mesenterium and divide it via the fan and steel incision.

So this was the original setup that was used in some of the earlier papers. So we looked at trying to simplify this to reduce a procedure to simple, reproducible steps, taking advantage of the anatomy of the mobilized colon, building on existing procedures, using fewer ports, and rethinking the site of the extraction incision.

So our basic building blocks we already had, right colon, left colon, and being able to do a rectal dissection. So we tried this with four ports in a standard diamond shape. The right lower quadrant port actually becomes the stoma site, so we don't need a separate incision. And as for right colon and left colon, we used a peri-umbilical incision.

The surgeon has mobilized and transected the rectum, positioning the entire colon and rectum as a midline structure centered beneath the umbilicus. A small peri-umbilical incision is used instead of a fan and steel incision for extraction.
So this then becomes the total number of incisions, as you'll see here, This is the setup in the operating room.

So a four-port technique reduced to five simple steps. We do the left colon, we do the right colon, we do the dissection of the rectum, and in its most simple form in a patient with normal body mass index, and in fact this can be done with patients with a BMI up to thirty, the entire colon and rectum with the mesentery still intact can be instructed through a peri-umbilical incision
The surgeon divides the entire mesentery extracorporeally, utilizing vessel sealing instruments and staplers to efficiently separate the mesentery from the colon and rectum.
This is the more complex version of it, with each of those five steps broken down into the individual steps within that, but I think the five-step approach helps to think about it in simpler fashion.
May I have the first video, please?
The surgeon begins the procedure on the left side of the abdomen, initiating the dissection process.
The left ureter is visible as the surgeon employs a lateral to medial approach during the open colectomy procedure.
The surgeon approaches the descending colon, moving proximally towards the splenic flexure, using a technique that avoids taking vessels intercorporeally.

The surgeon demonstrates two techniques for mobilizing the splenic flexure: detaching the omentum from the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure in slimmer patients, or accessing the lesser sac and removing the omentum along with the splenic flexure in heavier patients.
After fully mobilizing the splenic flexure, the surgeon shifts focus to the right colon, visually identifying the right ureter.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection, scoring the peritoneum around the base of the cecum and terminal ileum to access the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon uses a BOVI electrocautery device to perform dissection, creating a clean plane without tearing or pulling on the tissues.
The surgeon gently sweeps the filmy tissues along the right lateral peritoneal reflection to expose the correct dissection plane.
The surgeon gradually moves the dissection cephalad and medially, exposing the entire right retroperitoneum during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter, duodenum, and inferior vena cava while approaching the hepatic flexure. The dissection plane shifts medially as the surgeon progresses towards the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon carefully avoids dissection near the right kidney while mobilizing the right colon. In the right side up Trendelenburg position, the medial attachments of the terminal ileum are dissected up to the duodenum to achieve maximum mobility for the creation of the ilioanal pouch.
The surgeon elevates and divides the hepatocolic attachments near the hepatic flexure, completing the dissection of the retroperitoneum beneath these structures.
The hepatic flexure is positioned below the level of the umbilicus, indicating that the right colon is adequately mobilized for extraction. The surgeon then begins dissection in the pelvic region.

The surgeon opens the dissection plane, revealing the left ureter in the left lower quadrant of the visual field. The left pararectal tissues are scored to gain entry into the presacral space. The rectum is then elevated using cautery, maintaining a bloodless plane.

The surgeon uses simple cautery to maintain the correct dissection plane, applying tension on the rectum. The dissection proceeds along the left lateral pararectal attachments before moving to the right side of the rectum.

The right ureter is visible in the right lower quadrant of the surgical field. The surgeon scores the right pararectal tissues to enter the presacral space, where previous dissection has been completed from the left side.
The surgeon applies tension to the right pararectal tissues and continues the dissection in the presacral space, maintaining the correct anatomical plane.

Again, as Steve had noted earlier in his talk, in a female patient, it's often easier to work in the wider pelvis, but the uterus can sometimes act to be a little obstructive. So it can be elevated either using a Keith needle through the abdominal wall and placing a transcutaneous stitch either through the tip of the fundus of the uterus or even two stitches going through the bare areas of the broad ligament.

The surgeon carefully delineates the posterior wall of the vagina, ensuring not to damage it, especially in patients with a rectocele. After completing the posterior and bilateral dissection, tension is placed anteriorly to facilitate the completion of the dissection.

The surgeon uses a sizer or rigid proctoscope in the rectum to apply traction and delineate the tissue planes. A stapler is utilized through the right lower quadrant port to assist in this challenging part of the procedure.
The surgeon applies significant traction on the rectum with assistance from the perineum, causing a rim of tissue to retract into the pelvic floor when released.

The surgeon applies multiple staples, creating a visible staple line that cones down into the anal canal. The staple line is positioned at the top of the anal canal, leaving only one to two centimeters of mucosa in the proximal half.

The surgeon exteriorizes the colon and rectum through a peri-umbilical incision, positioning them as midline structures. The pouch is then created by utilizing the base of the small bowel mesentery beneath the incision, ensuring adequate length for the pouch construction.
The surgeon exteriorizes the entire small bowel to visually inspect for any signs of Crohn's disease in a patient with presumed ulcerative colitis.

The final incisions include a peri-umbilical extraction site and two five-millimeter ports at the ileostomy site. A suprapubic port is used to bring out a drain, resulting in a cosmetic appearance post-ileostomy closure.
To return to that issue of dysplasia, can we still address that laparoscopically? Of course we can, but not using such a simplified procedure.

It requires division of the vessels and mesentery intracorporeally at the base of the vessels. In a patient with low-grade dysplasia, there's anywhere from a ten to eighteen percent chance of there already being a cancer within the specimen. not necessarily at the site of dysplasia. And in high-grade dysplasia, up to a forty percent chance of also having a concurrent cancer. So you can't say that you can only take the vessels at the base in the area where the dysplasia was seen.
The surgeon performs an oncologic resection of the mesentery, removing the entire colon. A mucosectomy is carried out in a standard fashion, ensuring complete removal of the mucosal layer.

Can we go to the second video, please? So in someone in whom we've taken the entire mesentery intracorporeally, we then don't need a peri-umbilical extraction incision, because as you can see here, the entire colon and rectum can be brought out through the ileostomy site.

So this is a more complex procedure, doing it this way because of having to take the mesentery intracorporeally, but it does avoid that peri-umbilical extraction incision. So a little bit more to come out.
The surgeon demonstrates the extraction of a bulky rectum, affected by disease and inflammation, through a standard-sized ileostomy site.
The surgeon creates a J-pouch in standard fashion, ensuring it extends down to the pubis.

The surgeon brings up the ileostomy and places a clamp across the small bowel. The pneumoperitoneum is reestablished, revealing the staple line positioned well. The surgeon checks the cut edge of the small bowel mesentery for twists by tracing it up to the duodenum and ensures no loops of small bowel have slipped beneath the mesentery. The anastomosis is then created.
The surgeon matures the ileostomy, finalizing its formation at the abdominal surface.

So I think, in summary, I think from what we have available in the literature, this does appear to be safe, doing protoclectomy and J-patch laparoscopically. There do appear to be some benefits in short-term outcomes. Another thing we're seeing is reduction in adhesions and certainly improved cosmesis. And as far as the technique goes, I think it's important to be familiar with the open technique in order to have a backup plan and to break the procedure into simple, reproducible steps. Thank you for your attention.
